name: Perry Ellis International (PEI) - Customer Service Voice Bot 2.0
context: Customer service modernization for the ASI division.
outcome: Modernized B2B customer service by automating complex inquiries across inventory, pricing, order tracking, and shipping. The system targeted 80% automation, reduced live agent load, and supported English and Spanish interactions.
architecture: Moved from a rigid Dialogflow-only implementation to a multi-agent architecture using LangGraph and LangChain.
Hosted the agentic system on a multi-node Google Kubernetes Engine cluster with multi-zone redundancy.
Used Vertex AI Gemini for generative responses and Vector Search for RAG-based FAQ retrieval.
Connected securely to legacy on-premise ERP systems including Oracle and RepSpark through VPN/private tunnels for real-time lookups.
Implemented a dedicated PII redaction layer before persistence or logging.
scale: Designed for high availability and handled approximately 1,000 calls per month.
lessons: Intent-classifier bots become brittle as workflows grow; a supervisor agent with modular sub-agents made debugging and scaling easier.
Langfuse-style tracing is critical for production LLM systems because hallucinations and integration failures need granular input/output visibility.
Sensitive workflows need architectural hard-stops. The bot stayed read-only for actions such as payment routing and escalated to humans when required.

name: Nexon - AI Sandbox Azure Deployment
context: Secure AI experimentation environment on Microsoft Azure.
outcome: Delivered a functional AI Sandbox on Azure so the business unit could test and validate AI models without affecting production systems.
architecture: Used Azure-native cloud services for containerized deployment.
Created an isolated sandbox that mirrored production-grade security constraints.
Kept the design portable across clouds by standardizing infrastructure-as-code practices.
scale: Built as a dedicated business-unit environment for safe AI model experimentation and validation.
lessons: Multi-cloud delivery reinforced the value of Terraform and portable infrastructure patterns.
A sandbox only works if it mirrors production network constraints such as VPC design, firewalls, and access controls closely enough to catch real deployment issues.

name: Perry Ellis - GitLab DevSecOps Modernization
context: Security-first modernization of CI/CD, infrastructure, and service delivery practices.
outcome: Moved the organization toward a security-first CI/CD model, reducing manual overhead and strengthening alignment with ISO 27001 and NIST-oriented controls.
architecture: Centralized source control and CI/CD on GitLab.
Enforced signed commits, stronger secret handling, and secure pipeline controls.
Used Google Secret Manager for secret management.
Embedded static and dynamic security checks inside the deployment pipeline.
scale: Designed as a foundational delivery platform for multiple application and infrastructure workflows.
lessons: Shift-left security works best when security checks are built into the pipeline instead of handled after deployment.
Workload identity reduced risk more effectively than static service account keys because it removed long-lived credentials from the delivery path.

name: AI Scrum Master Elisa Rollout
context: Internal process improvement integrated into project delivery workflows.
outcome: Automated routine scrum support, backlog flow, sprint reporting, meeting progress insights, pre-reads, and summary reports.
architecture: Integrated AI-assisted workflow support into team ceremonies and project reporting.
Connected operational context from tools such as Jira and Slack.
Used centralized configuration management to reduce breakage from process and meeting changes.
scale: Rolled into active project delivery to improve recurring team operations and visibility.
lessons: Onboarding an AI agent is a product experience; data quality and tool ingestion determine adoption.
Internal automation is vulnerable to configuration drift when meeting links, ceremonies, or workflows change.
Real-time active communication from an AI Scrum Master was more useful for team momentum than static reporting alone.
